rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical cathodic reductive hydrodimerization of methyl cinnamate (and related phenylacrylates) on a boron-doped diamond (BDD) electrode to give neolignan-type C-C coupled dimers.
electrochemical_role: Cathodic one-electron reduction of the methyl cinnamate (phenylacrylate) C=C to a benzylic/stabilized radical anion (after protonation, a benzylic radical), which dimerizes to the hydrodimer.

key_intermediates.name: radical anion / benzylic radical of methyl cinnamate after 1e- reduction and protonation

electron_flow: The BDD cathode delivers one electron to methyl cinnamate (1a) generating a radical anion that is protonated to a benzylic radical at the β-carbon; two such radicals couple to give the hydrodimer 2, while undesired hydrolysis to 1b is suppressed by using a phosphate-buffered catholyte.

probe_reagents.name: electrode material variation (GC, Pt, Mg vs BDD)
probe_reagents.observation: Pt and Mg cathodes gave only H2 evolution and recovery of 1a; GC gave dimer 2 (34%, dr 74:26) plus cyclic product E-3 (25%) and 41% 1a; BDD selectively produced hydrodimer 2 in 85% yield.

probe_reagents.name: phosphate buffer (catholyte)
probe_reagents.observation: Suppresses hydrolysis of ester 1a to 1b and improves selectivity for dimer 2.

notes: On other cathodes (Hg, Cu, Pb, Zn, Sn, Ag) the major products from cinnamate reductions are reported to be cyclic Dieckmann-type products (type 3), whereas BDD uniquely favors the open-chain hydrodimer 2. The proposed mechanism (Figure 1, right) is drawn but not interrogated with radical traps, KIE, CV, or DFT in this paper.
